data.title: Will Philippines favour Eurofighter Typhoon for its military upgrade?
data.description:
An Italian defence company’s push to sell Eurofighter Typhoon jets to the Philippines could open the door to broader cooperation between Rome and Manila, with analysts calling the aircraft a promising choice for the country’s military modernisation.
Leonardo, a government-linked company, said it was committed to investing in the Philippines’ defence capabilities for the long term and offered to provide training, technology transfer and related facilities as part of the deal.
The company is promoting the Eurofighter – a multi-role combat aircraft that it has co-developed through a consortium with European plane maker Airbus and British aerospace group BAE Systems – to countries in the Indo-Pacific.
Among them, Bangladesh on Tuesday signed a letter of intent with Leonardo, in a first step towards buying the Eurofighter. The country is targeting to acquire up to 16 such aircraft, according to media reports.
Leonardo is partly owned by the Italian government, which has a 30.2 per cent stake in the company.
The cost of each Eurofighter is reportedly estimated at US$117 million, making it one of the world’s most expensive fighter aircraft.
The Philippine military is planning to acquire advanced multi-role fighter aircraft as part of a US$35 billion modernisation programme.

“Leonardo, as part of the Eurofighter consortium, stands ready to support the Philippines as it moves towards a more capable, self-reliant and unmatched deterrence posture,” said Tommaso Pani, senior vice-president of marketing and sales in Leonardo’s aeronautics division, in a statement on Tuesday.
The company’s proposal comes as Italy and the Philippines continue to hold talks to finalise a defence cooperation agreement, which Davide Giglio, the Italian ambassador in Manila, said in September was developing “very positively”.
Officials from Leonardo and representatives from Italy’s state-owned export credit agency SACE met with Giglio and Philippine defence officials this week to discuss the Eurofighter offer.
Leonardo is proposing technology transfer, training for Filipino engineers and pilots, and the Philippine defence industry’s integration into the Eurofighter ecosystem. It has also raised the possibility of establishing a Eurofighter logistics and training hub in the Philippines for maintenance, repair and overhaul as well as simulation activities.
This Week in Asia has reached out to the Philippine military for its response to Leonardo’s proposal.
With its agility, speed and high-altitude performance, the Eurofighter was a promising option for the Philippine military, Chester Cabalza, president and CEO of the International Development and Security Cooperation, told This Week in Asia.
The offer from Leonardo would also include the combat-proven Meteor Beyond Visual Range Air-to-Air missiles, Storm Shadow cruise missiles and Marte-ER anti-ship missiles, which would boost the Philippine air force’s deterrence capabilities, Cabalza added.
The deal is worth considering as the Philippine industry’s involvement in the Eurofighter ecosystem and training for local engineers and pilots would help develop the country’s indigenous defence expertise and create highly skilled jobs in systems integration and avionics, according to Cabalza.

“The high-end capabilities of the Typhoon allow the Philippines to establish a credible deterrence against potential threats by patrolling its borders effectively,” he said.
Beyond their defence cooperation agreement, which reflects a “pragmatic investment in deterrence and resilience against regional threats”, Italy also wants to forge closer ties with the Philippines in trade and other areas, according to Calbaza.
Noting the long-standing relationship between the Philippines and Italy, where over 165,000 Filipinos had formed a community, Calbaza said Rome considered the Southeast Asian country a gateway to the Indo-Pacific region.
“Italy views the Indo-Pacific region as being as strategically important as the Mediterranean Sea for global trade and stability,” he added.
Matteo Piasentini, a geopolitical analyst for the Italian think tank Geopolitica.info and an international relations lecturer at the University of the Philippines Diliman, told This Week in Asia that Rome’s engagement with Manila reflected its “enlarged Mediterranean” concept.
Like other European countries in the Mediterranean region, Italy has a huge stake in global trade and desires seamless connections between the sea lanes of Europe and Asia, according to Piasentini. As such, it could not ignore destabilising developments in the South China Sea that might disrupt trade flows, he added.
“As is the case for many other middle-ranking powers, Italy’s strategic interests are closely intertwined with the interests of its defence industry. A successful big-ticket sale [of the Eurofighter] would further align the priorities of Rome and Manila, ultimately benefiting both countries.”

data.title: Hong Kong’s former commerce minister Gregory So Kam-Leung dead at 67
data.description:
Hong Kong’s former commerce minister Gregory So Kam-leung has died at the age of 67.
It was understood that So, who served as the city’s Secretary for Commerce and Economic Development between 2011 and 2017, had been in hospital for some time before he died on Saturday morning.
He oversaw a number of thorny issues during his tenure, including broadcast regulation, intellectual property protection, and a controversial bid for a free-to-air broadcast licence by outspoken businessman and media executive Ricky Wong Wai-kay.
So, the former chairman of the Democratic Alliance for the Betterment and Progress of Hong Kong (DAB), first joined the government in 2008 as the Commerce and Economic Development Bureau’s undersecretary.
His death, which came as a surprise to many in the city, was first revealed by former city leader Leung Chun-ying, who worked with So during his own tenure as the city’s chief executive between 2012 and 2017.
More to follow …

data.title: Arsenal legend Silva defends World Cup expansion, fears Brazil aren’t good enough to win
data.description:
Brazil and Arsenal legend Gilberto Silva believes Fifa is moving with the times by expanding the World Cup and introducing a quadrennial 32-team club equivalent.
A sprawling World Cup finals next year in the United States, Canada and Mexico will feature 48 teams, up from the 32 that contested the past seven editions.
Silva, a former defensive midfielder, was ever-present for Brazil when they won the most recent of their five World Cups in 2002.
Speaking at an event in Hong Kong organised by CSTS Enterprises and supported by Connexus Travel, which is the official sales agent for 2026 World Cup hospitality packages across China, Silva said he was not worried about the absence of jeopardy in a two-week group phase that will eliminate only 16 nations.
It will take nearly a month to whittle those 48 teams down to 16, and the tournament itself will need almost six weeks to complete.
“Whenever anything changes, especially at such a major tournament, we question the potential quality of the games and the length of the competition, and fear it might not be as interesting,” Silva said.
“It’s a great opportunity for countries who’ve never gone to a World Cup, or not qualified for a long time. Every team will be so excited to be there, and it will bring more fans. I want to be positive about it.”

Part of Fifa’s Technical Study Group for last summer’s new-look Club World Cup, Silva gave the competition an unqualified thumbs up.
“It allowed Brazilian teams, and others like Mamelodi Sundowns [from South Africa], to face the best sides in the world,” said Silva, adding that he would have welcomed the extra games as a player.
“It’s a very short career, and you have to enjoy it as much as you can,” he said. “I’m a very fortunate person; I had a season [2002-03] when I played more than 50 games, but I never complained. I loved every minute.”

The 49-year-old said the current Brazil squad had “good players but not a good team”.
He rated Argentina, the Netherlands, France, Portugal and Spain as more likely champions next summer, but urged Carlo Ancelotti’s players to embrace the pressure of trying to end Brazil’s 24-year drought.
“Representing more than 200 million people is one of the greatest things you can do,” Silva said.
“It’s like soldiers going to war for their country, but some soldiers don’t come back alive. You can come back from the World Cup a hero, but if you don’t win people will still always remember how you behaved, sacrificed yourself and represented them. You have to leave everything on the field.”
Following his appointment in May, head coach Ancelotti recalled his old Real Madrid midfielder Casemiro, with the 33-year-old also firmly back in favour at current club Manchester United.
“We suffered a bit without him … you need a player to complement [creative midfielder] Bruno Guimaraes, and to sacrifice for the team,” Silva said. “There are not many holding midfielders left, but Casemiro has been outstanding for Brazil. He also brings leadership and knows how to handle the pressure. I was happy when he came back.”
An integral member of Arsenal’s invincible Premier League champions from 2003-04, Silva was among those with Gunners allegiances who wanted the club to recruit a striker last summer.
They duly spent a reported £64 million (US$85.6 million) on Viktor Gyokeres, who has laboured following his arrival from Sporting Lisbon.
“He’s still trying to find himself in the team and has to adapt to the way Arsenal play,” Silva said. “I hope he’s going to get there.”

data.title: Singapore’s Pritam Singh, Mediacorp apologise over court contempt in CNA interview
data.description:
Singapore’s opposition leader Pritam Singh has apologised to the court for statements he made on a CNA programme while CNA’s parent company, Mediacorp, also issued an apology and took down the interview.
“I accept that my statements may be construed to be in contempt of court, including to impugn the integrity, propriety or impartiality of the court,” Singh wrote on social media on Saturday.
“Given that, I wholly and unreservedly apologise to the court and will avoid making comments to the same or similar effect in future.”
Mediacorp said on Saturday that it had taken down the interview after being notified by the Attorney General’s Chambers that it was in contempt of court.
The interview for an episode of CNA’s The Assembly was recorded in July and aired on November 5. The Assembly series features interviews with newsmakers conducted by a group of neurodivergent individuals.
In the episode, Singh responded to a question on how he felt about being called a liar.

The Workers’ Party (WP) chief was found guilty in February for lying to a parliamentary committee and fined S$14,000 (US$10,800) for two charges linked to how he handled former WP MP Raeesah Khan’s lie in parliament.
He was found to have given false testimony to a parliamentary committee that was looking into the conduct of Khan, who had admitted to making up a story about accompanying a sexual assault victim to the police station.
Singh appealed against the conviction and the appeal was heard on November 4. On December 4, the court dismissed his appeal.
In a statement on Saturday, Mediacorp said: “On November 5, Mediacorp aired the interview given by Mr Pritam Singh with The Assembly on CNA. This interview was recorded in July 2025. In the interview, Mr Singh made some statements regarding the case relating to Ms Raeesah Khan, where Mr Singh had been found guilty by the Magistrate’s Court in February of lying on oath to the Committee of Privileges. The interview was aired a day after Mr Singh’s appeal against his conviction was heard, with the court reserving its judgment.”
It added: “We accept that the publication of the interview, which contained the statements by Mr Singh regarding the case relating to Ms Raeesah Khan, is in contempt of court. We wholly and unreservedly apologise to the court.
“We have taken down the interview from our platforms and channels and will avoid publishing matters that are in contempt of court in future.”
This article was first published by CNA
Additional reporting by SCMP’s Asia desk

data.title: Who is Kim Woo-bin, the steely-eyed star of K-dramas Our Blues and Genie, Make a Wish?
data.description:
Earlier this year, Kim Woo-bin graced the small screen in Netflix’s splashy fantasy romance Genie, Make a Wish, reuniting with his Uncontrollably Fond co-star Bae Suzy and Kim Eun-sook, the writer behind The Heirs – one of the actor’s earliest projects.
While the series received mixed reviews, it was nevertheless a compelling showcase for Kim’s chiselled features and magnetic screen aura.
Yet Kim’s biggest moment of 2025 is still to come: on December 20, he is set to tie the knot with girlfriend Shin Min-a. The actors shared the screen in the ensemble hit Our Blues (2022), although their relationship predates that project by several years.
Kim was born in Seoul, though he spent his formative years in Jeonju, a central city renowned for its culinary scene, where his father runs a cafe. He attended Jeonju University, which later invited him to be an honorary ambassador.

Why we love him
With his considerable height, steely eyes and sharp cheekbones, Kim was ideally suited for the runway and spent the first few years of his career as a model – a background that came in handy for his first film role in 2012, when he featured briefly in the fashion-themed crime comedy Runway Cop.
Kim’s distinct visage caught the eye of casting directors. After transitioning to acting, he specialised in playing tough guys and rebellious characters.
However, Kim proved he was a fighter off-screen as well. In the spring of 2017, just as his career was hitting its peak, he was diagnosed with nasopharyngeal cancer. While most actors go on hiatus in Korea to complete their obligatory military service, Kim was forced to step away to fight a graver battle.

To focus on his recovery, he dropped all commitments, including a lead role in a planned Korean remake of the Hong Kong crime thriller Overheard, which was to be directed by Choi Dong-hoon.
Three years later, Kim announced his triumphant return, once again partnering with director Choi for the two-part blockbuster Alienoid, the first instalment of which arrived in 2022.
The star-making roles
Though not his first role, Kim gained exposure in the 2013 instalment of the long-running School anthology franchise, a proven launch pad for young stars. Among his fellow students in the series were Shin Hye-sun and Lee Jong-suk, with whom he developed a close friendship.
Kim stayed in his school uniform for his next role that year, when he was bumped up to the main cast for the smash hit The Heirs, alongside Lee Min-ho and Park Shin-hye. He memorably plays Choi Young-do, a hotel group heir and bully who steadily begins to change his ways when love opens his eyes.

During the initial broadcast of The Heirs, Kim also launched his career as a film lead, taking on one of the main roles in the gangland sequel Friend: The Great Legacy. He plays a convict who is befriended by a gang boss in prison, with whom he joins forces on the outside.
The iconic parts
After high school romance and big-screen action noir, Kim extended his range to youth comedy in the surprise hit Twenty (2015), from future Extreme Job director Lee Byeong-heon. The film follows a trio of best friends who have just graduated from high school, including Kim’s layabout playboy.
In 2016, Kim returned with a small-screen hit when he joined forces with Bae Suzy for the first time in the romantic drama Uncontrollably Fond. Kim and Bae play school sweethearts from different backgrounds who meet again as adults.

Following his health hiatus, Kim made his comeback in the star-studded Our Blues, which also featured Lee Byung-hun, Uhm Jung-hwa and Han Ji-min among many other marquee names.
In this series scripted by Noh Hee-kyung, Kim plays a warm-hearted, Jeju Island-based ship’s captain who strikes up a cautious relationship with a feisty newcomer to the island, played by Han.
The unheralded performances
Kim’s career has overwhelmingly been marked by success, but his most colourful performance to date remains his multi-role turn in the expensive sci-fi misfire Alienoid.
Kim plays an alien android who takes on several human personas, each more entertaining and outrageously dressed than the last. Despite the many comedy veterans in the Alienoid ensemble, Kim surprised many with his range, providing much of the comic relief in both instalments of the series.

Another film worth seeking out is The Con Artists. A solid box office performer at the time of its release but largely forgotten now, the 2014 film was a fresh and lively caper action-thriller, anchored by Kim’s charismatic turn as a cocky safe-cracker.
Tomorrow’s turns
In the wake of Genie, Make a Wish, Kim appears set to return to the screen in the baseball-themed series Gift, which is based on a webtoon.
He will play Jeong Min-young, a professional baseball pitching coach who quits after developing supernatural abilities. His life takes a turn when he assumes the role of head coach at Deokcheon High School.
The series will reunite him with Kim Kyu-tae, who directed him in Our Blues and most recently helmed the Gong Yoo series The Trunk. The tvN show is expected to begin production next year.

data.title: Dick Van Dyke turns 100: 5 things to know about the Mary Poppins star
data.description:
Dick Van Dyke turns 100 today, on December 13, and the world is celebrating this milestone with him. The Mary Poppins icon and author of 100 Rules for Living to 100: An Optimist’s Guide to a Happy Life recently admitted to The New York Times that while he’s “super old ... sometimes I feel like I’m 15 again”.

The secret to ageing gracefully, Van Dyke says, is exercise, pets and playfulness. In a chat with Today’s Al Roker, the Emmy-winning actor also shared that his 54-year-old wife, Arlene Silver, “keeps [him] a teenager”. And, speaking of his legacy, the Disney legend mused, “The movies and the TV show [The Dick Van Dyke Show] kind of speak for me.”
Here are five interesting facts about the star.
His parents weren’t in showbiz

Dick Van Dyke was born in West Plains, Missouri, to Loren “Cookie” Van Dyke and Hazel Van Dyke. His father was a minor-league baseball player and musician who became a salesman to support his family, he told The Guardian. His mother was a stenographer. His younger brother, Jerry Van Dyke, was a comedian.
Before becoming an actor, Van Dyke served as a radio announcer in the US Army Air Corps during the Second World War and briefly ran an ad agency, according to CNN.
He once didn’t know his real birthday

In his memoir My Lucky Life In and Out of Show Business, Van Dyke revealed that just before turning 18, he was contemplating joining the US Air Force. That’s when his mother told him he was actually already 18 and that his date of birth had been pushed forward from December to March because he was born premature. However, his grandmother later disclosed that the date was altered because he was born out of wedlock – a scandal in 1925 and especially in their small town.
His was close with Walt Disney

During Van Dyke’s recent Vandy High Tea charity event, the Chitty Chitty Bang Bang star opened up about being “the last living person who knew the real Walt Disney”. He told guests, “We decided that emotionally we were both 13 years old,” adding, “He was a big kid, just so full of enthusiasm, and I just loved him.”
He gave up drinking and smoking in his 50s

While reminiscing about Disney, Van Dyke also explained that he had once been a heavy smoker. The centenarian then revealed that he gave up cigarettes and alcohol in his fifties when he realised he had an “addictive personality”. He sought treatment for his alcoholism, per People, but admitted that quitting smoking was “twice as hard”.
Regrets? He has a few

Van Dyke also told Al Roker that he regrets turning down the offer to play James Bond after Sean Connery. At the time, he felt he’d already established himself in a certain type of film and that audiences wouldn’t appreciate him taking on the role.
He also revealed during the Vandy High Tea that he regrets passing on projects with Cary Grant and Sophia Loren, the latter because his agent turned the opportunity down. The Vantastix a capella group performer “never forgave” him for the lapse.

data.title: China’s green tech revolution will outlast geopolitics with scale and cost: Arif Aga
data.description:
The global green energy transition is less about geopolitics and more about economic reality, according to Arif Aga, director of global renewable engineering consultancy SgurrEnergy.
For many nations seeking to adopt clean energy, their priority remains the practical pursuit of lowering costs, a reality that has entrenched China’s leading role in the global clean power supply.
According to the renewable energy technical adviser, the vast scale of Chinese manufacturing and its rapid enhancement of technologies are the catalysts for the industry’s dramatic cost reductions.
“Geopolitics is a temporary thing. It is all about politics and politicians. Eventually things will be different,” Aga told the Post in an interview this month.
“If any country has to scale up its renewable energy penetration to lower the carbon footprint, they have to have a dependency on a partner who has a long track record that would ensure projects are durable,” he said.
“Technological changes are happening at a very high pace. An established player will have an upper edge to meet the technological change requirements or embed the technological change design to the system.”
“With the scales going up, China definitely is here to stay for a long, long time,” he added.
Aga, who leads the India-headquartered company, pointed to China’s dominance in the renewable supply chain – from raw materials to new innovations.


“We have worked in more than 55 countries and out of them, Chinese modules and components are being used in 52 countries – be it the United States, Africa, Southeast Asia, India and the Middle East,” he said.
Describing some of these renewable projects as “shipped by China, executed in Southeast Asia”, Aga said his firm had taken part in initial projects in countries such as Malaysia, Indonesia, Vietnam and the Philippines.
Southeast Asian countries have seen their renewable energy capacity more than double over the last decade. But they also need to modernise electricity grids to sustain their economic boom and unlock the full potential of renewable energy, according to independent global energy think tank Ember.
The region was also the largest photovoltaic (PV) production hub making solar panels outside China in 2024, with direct investments from large Chinese manufacturers speeding up the expansion of manufacturing capacity, according to the International Energy Agency.
“These investments were primarily intended to geographically diversify solar PV manufacturing to serve markets with trade restrictions on Chinese imports, particularly the United States,” the agency said in its annual renewables report released in October.
In addition to designing and engineering projects including solar, wind and energy storage, Aga said the consultancy also inspected component factories, which gave him first-hand experience working with Chinese PV module manufacturers.
He said around 60 per cent of the firm’s inspection services were conducted in Chinese factories and 35 per cent in India.
“Chinese participation in renewable energy has been remarkable,” Aga said. “The edge that Chinese companies have [for example, with] PV modules is the scalability.”

“There is a lot of feedback that we as consultants give for technological enhancements to reduce costs. The way Chinese companies pick up or accept our feedback and execute it – I do not see any other country doing that,” he said.
He explained that the rapid adoption of technological enhancements was linked to the sheer size of the Chinese manufacturing sector.
For instance, a manufacturer producing 50 gigawatts (GW) of modules will have a research and development budget proportional to its larger production capacity and revenue, compared to a 5GW company, allowing it to invest significantly more in innovation, he said.
“Chinese companies have that upper edge because of scalability and their larger revenues to do all kinds of enhancements. That is commendable,” Aga added.
He said lowering costs had always been the key priority for green energy projects, pointing to the potential for Southeast Asia to adopt more floating solar plants, given their vast areas of water, to cut costs.
“Implementation cost across the geography has to be lower than the conventional [energy sources], only then it would support energy transition,” Aga said.
“We as global citizens have to look at saving our planet and making the planet green. That is what we all want, but at what cost? Eventually, everything boils down to the cost.
“Unless and until it is commercially viable, banks are not going to fund the project. Developers, irrespective of how big [they are], they would eventually like to make money.”

On the flip side of the economic coin, the International Energy Agency warned about the “financial sustainability of equipment manufacturers” in its report released in October.
“Major solar PV and wind manufacturers have reported large losses despite surging global installations,” it said.
“In China, solar PV prices are down over 60 per cent since 2023 due to supply glut of modules and competition for market share. This has reduced the margins of the largest manufacturers to -10 per cent, with cumulative losses reaching almost US$5 billion since the beginning of 2024.”
It said continued growth in overcapacity had driven PV module prices to record lows in 2024.
According to the IEA, the average annual global wholesale price saw a decline of almost 45 per cent year on year to US$0.09/W. This price stabilised at that level in the first half of 2025.
“This price is below the production cost for most manufacturers, including leading Chinese firms, and is widely considered unsustainable in the medium to long term,” it said, adding that net profit margins of major PV manufacturers became negative in 2024 and early 2025.
Trade restrictions also meant actual PV module prices varied across countries. US-imported modules cost three times the global average, while India-produced modules were 70 per cent higher.
The issue was also raised in February by research firm BloombergNEF which noted: “China’s clean technology manufacturing overcapacity has led to rising protectionism in the form of import tariffs by countries to avoid cheap imports upending their own energy markets.”

data.title: Primary school near Hong Kong estate destroyed in deadly blaze hopes to move
data.description:
The principal of a Hong Kong primary school opposite the scene of the deadly Tai Po fire has expressed hope that the institution could relocate, saying the horrifying view of the charred buildings will put parents off and affect enrolment.
Tai Po Baptist Public School head Siu Ting said the campus was still sealed off and there was currently no date set for when pupils might return.
The school’s students had been learning in makeshift classrooms at two public universities for the past two weeks and will attend classes at two other primary schools starting from next Monday and until at least the end of the school year.
Siu said she doubted that Tai Po Baptist Public School’s campus near Wang Fuk Court was still fit for educational purposes.
“Going back to our campus to attend classes is not an ideal arrangement for every stakeholder of the school,” Siu said.
School supervisor Tsang Ka-shek earlier said the nearby scene was too disturbing.
The school, which is just opposite the estate, is the only institution to remain cordoned off after the deadly inferno that broke out on November 26.

The fire left at least 160 people dead and another 79 injured after raging through seven of the housing complex’s eight blocks for about 43 hours.
Siu added that since it was still unclear whether Wang Fuk Court would be demolished and rebuilt, the uncertainty gave school management additional concerns about letting students return to the campus.
“We very much hope our school can be relocated,” she said.
The principal said some parents had already said they would switch to other schools if the students were told to return to the campus.
Siu said the school would face further challenges in January when parents would make their choices using the central allocation system to determine where their children would enrol for Primary One.
She said she hoped the government could provide a discretionary arrangement to the public school, given that such institutions were required to reach an enrolment threshold to receive funding or potentially face being axed.
The school’s wish to relocate elsewhere within Tai Po district was being conveyed to education authorities, she said.
“Our supervisor and incorporated management committee are still communicating this issue with the Education Bureau, but there is no magic. We have to wait,” Siu said.
The principal said the temporary relocation to two other primary schools could also pose challenges and expressed concerns that the presence of the additional students might affect others and create conflicts.

Tai Po Baptist Public School’s Primary Two and Four pupils, comprising eight classes, will move to NTWJWA Christian Remembrance of Grace Primary School.
The remaining pupils, comprising 18 classes, will go to Tai Po Government Primary School.
“We are not moving to vacant school premises. The schools where we are going to have students studying. Some of our teachers described it as lodging at someone else’s place,” Siu said.
“It is not only an issue of having a classroom. Students also have to get out of the classrooms and interact with others.
“We are afraid there will be conflicts among students and teachers. It will eventually affect students’ learning.”
Carol Chan Suk-yee, Tai Po Baptist Public School’s vice-principal, also said staff had not dared to bring too many things from the old location since they were using other institutions’ campuses.
The Education Bureau earlier said it would give HK$5 million (US$642,600) to the three schools affected by the fire to support them during the transition period.
Siu said the funds had been used to set up new offices at the two other schools and to renovate some rooms to suit Tai Po Baptist Public School’s needs.
In a reply to the Post, the bureau said it would assess the situation and consider all feasible options to help the school with its campus issues as soon as possible, adding that students’ interests were education authorities’ primary concern.
“[The bureau] will announce the arrangements in due course … and continue to maintain close contact with the school and provide necessary assistance,” a spokeswoman said.

data.title: Fire breaks out at flat in Hong Kong’s Tin Shui Wai, 1 injured and 200 evacuated
data.description:
A fire broke out at a flat in a public housing estate in Hong Kong’s Tin Shui Wai early on Saturday, leaving a man severely injured, with more than 200 residents evacuated.
Authorities received multiple calls at 3.43am on Saturday from residents reporting the blaze at Tin Chak Estate in Tin Shui Wai, with occasional sounds of explosions.
Firefighters rushed to the scene and rescued a man living in the flat on fire. He was found conscious but later fell into a coma after being rushed to Tuen Mun Hospital. He sustained burns on 20 per cent of his skin.
A total of 11 men and 12 women were evacuated by firefighters, while more than 200 others left their flats of their own accord.

The blaze was subsequently extinguished and residents returned to their flats.
The Fire Services Department said it was still investigating the cause of the blaze.

data.title: North Korea’s Kim reveals soldiers cleared mines for Russia, in rare public statement
data.description:
North Korea sent troops to clear mines in Russia’s Kursk region earlier this year, leader Kim Jong-un said in a speech carried on Saturday by state media, in a rare acknowledgement of the deadly tasks assigned to its soldiers there.
Pyongyang has sent thousands of troops to fight for Moscow as it presses ahead with its nearly four-year invasion of Ukraine, according to South Korean and Western intelligence agencies.
Analysts say Russia is giving North Korea financial aid, military technology, food and energy supplies in return, allowing the diplomatically isolated nation to sidestep tough international sanctions on its nuclear and missile programmes.
Last month, Russia’s Defence Ministry said North Korean troops who helped Russia repel a major Ukrainian incursion into the Kursk region were now playing an important role in clearing the area of mines.

Under a mutual defence pact between the two countries, North Korea last year sent some 14,000 soldiers to fight alongside Russia in Kursk, and more than 6,000 were killed.
Hailing the return of an engineering regiment, Kim said that they wrote “letters to their hometowns and villages at breaks of the mine-clearing hours”, according to the Korean Central News Agency (KCNA).
The regiment suffered the “heart-rending loss of nine lives” during the 120-day deployment that started in August, Kim said in a speech at a welcome ceremony on Friday, KCNA reported.
He awarded the deceased soldiers state honours to “add eternal lustre” to their bravery.
“All of you, both officers and soldiers, displayed mass heroism overcoming unimaginable mental and physical burdens almost every day,” Kim said.


The troops had been able to “work a miracle of turning a vast area of danger zone into a safe and secure one in a matter of less than three months”.
Saying that the regiment had showed “absolute loyalty” to the party and the state, Kim praised the troops’ political indoctrination, discipline and unity, calling their performance a model for the armed forces.
North Korea only confirmed in April that it had deployed troops to support Russia and that its soldiers had been killed in combat.
Images released by KCNA showed a smiling Kim embracing returned soldiers, some in wheelchairs, at the grand ceremony in Pyongyang on Friday.
Additional reporting by Reuters
